does: This paper applies mechanistic interpretability techniques to understand how the Particle Transformer neural network classifies jets in particle physics. The authors systematically ablate and patch components of a model trained on Top Quark Tagging to isolate a minimal six-head circuit that preserves most of the original model's classification performance. Using zero ablation, path patching with corruption strategies, and linear probing of the residual stream, they reveal a sparse interpretable structure with a clear source-relay-readout organization: one early head serves as the causal source, middle-layer heads relay information by attending to pairwise substructure, and a late head aggregates the signal for classification. Linear probes demonstrate that the residual stream aligns preferentially with energy correlator representations, connecting learned representations to known physics concepts.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability framework, previously applied to language and vision models, into the domain of high-energy physics neural networks. It builds on prior mechanistic interpretability research that identifies sparse circuits and causal components within deep networks, adapting techniques like path patching and ablation studies developed for transformer analysis. The paper responds to growing interest in understanding what neural networks learn about physical systems and how their internal representations relate to domain-specific feature spaces like energy correlators used in particle physics. It connects to broader efforts in physics-informed machine learning that seek interpretability through alignment with established physics bases and domain knowledge.
enables: This work opens directions for mechanistic understanding of physics-focused neural networks and could support validation of learned models in safety-critical physics applications. The demonstrated link between learned representations and energy correlator bases suggests a pathway for extracting physical interpretability from black-box networks, potentially enabling more trustworthy deployment in experiments. Future work could extend these methods to other particle physics tasks and architectures, develop techniques to guide training toward interpretable circuits, or use mechanistic insights to design more efficient networks. The approach also demonstrates how interpretability can verify that neural networks learn physically meaningful features rather than exploiting spurious correlations, supporting broader adoption of deep learning in domains requiring high confidence and auditability.
